The major- and trace-element characteristics of the mineralized beds (e.g., Al2O3/TiO2, Eu/Eu*, Nb/Yb, and REY distribution patterns) are comparable to ELIP alkaline rocks, whereas non-mineralized beds are closer to ELIP basalts. Zircon U-Pb ages from the mineralized beds cluster at 260.9−257.4 Ma, consistent with late-stage ELIP magmatism, while a small number of older detrital zircons are mainly Neoproterozoic in age. The former records εHf(t) values ranging from −15.71 to +11.37 and TDM1 model ages of 1498−438 Ma, overall showing within-plate affinities, with only a minor arc-related zircon population that may reflect hydrothermal alteration. Whole-rock 87Sr/86Sr(t) values of 0.70259−0.70606 and εNd(t) values of −3.58 to +1.75 show ranges comparable to those of Nb-Ta-mineralized alkaline rocks in the ELIP interior [87Sr/86Sr(t) = 0.70004−0.70424; εNd(t) = 0.4−2.7]. The mineralized mudstones reflect a mixed provenance, dominated by alkaline volcaniclastic input during late-stage ELIP activity, with additional contributions from weathered Precambrian basement rocks beneath ELIP volcanic edifices and, to a lesser extent, Neoproterozoic uplands along the Yangtze Block margins. A minor input also came from contemporaneous arc-related volcanism linked to Paleo-Tethyan subduction, which may have increased upward through the Permian sequence. These results highlight that the enrichment of critical metals in volcanic-influenced sedimentary basins was primarily driven by plume-related alkaline volcaniclastics, with additional contributions from crustal and arc sources.

item.title: A central position for the South China Block in Rodinia: New evidence from a non-subduction origin of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode mafic–ultramafic rocks
item.dc:creator: Chutian Shu
Xi Zhu
Zheng-Xiang Li
Jingyu Wang
Zhuoying Zhang
Xiaoping Long
item.description: The South China Block (SCB) was formed through the amalgamation of the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks along the Sibao orogen during the early Neoproterozoic assembly of the Rodinia supercontinent. Although possessing records of both the assembly and breakup of Rodinia, the position of the SCB in Rodinia remains debated, mainly due to disagreement on the tectonic settings of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism along the orogen. The magmatism has been interpreted either as subduction-related, placing South China on the margin of Rodinia, or as plume-related, suggesting a central position linking Australia and Laurentia. In this study, we investigate a gabbro-peridotite complex in the central Sibao orogen, with the peridotite showing cumulate textures and the gabbro giving a zircon U Pb crystallization age of 812.8 ± 5.7 Ma. These samples, together with a compilation of 560 published mafic–ultramafic and granitic samples within the 827 ± 22 Ma episode from the Sibao orogen, define cumulate lines of descent characterized by a continuous decline in Mg# with increasing SiO 2 , rather than the Z -shaped Mg#-SiO2 trend diagnostic of hydrous, subduction-related magmas, which is consistent with crystallization from anhydrous melts. This indicates that the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism originated from a dry mantle source, inconsistent with an active subduction setting. Mantle potential temperatures estimated from near-primary high-MgO basalts (MgO up to 17.5 wt%, Mg# up to 75, showing equilibrium with mantle) within this episode exceed 1525 °C, significantly above the ambient mantle potential temperature expected at ~827 Ma (1350–1420 °C), pointing to an anomalously hot mantle source. Initial 187 Os/ 188 Os isotopes of the studied cumulative peridotites (0.1376–0.1642) and coeval mafic–ultramafic rocks (0.1100–0.4191) indicate assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) processes involving asthenospheric components (plume), continental lithospheric mantle, and crustal material. This is consistent with plume underplating and interaction with the pre-existing subduction-modified lithosphere and overlying crust. Coeval plume-related magmatism is also documented elsewhere within the South China Block beyond the Sibao orogen, e.g., the Bikou flood basalts, further supporting a regional-scale thermal anomaly. Collectively, these observations are best explained by mantle plume activity, supporting a central position for the South China Block in Rodinia. Published: 2026-03-09T16:40:39+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.577 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chutian Shu; Xi Zhu; Zheng-Xiang Li; Jingyu Wang; Zhuoying Zhang; Xiaoping Long

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123360

Relevance: 0.577 (consider)

The South China Block (SCB) was formed through the amalgamation of the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks along the Sibao orogen during the early Neoproterozoic assembly of the Rodinia supercontinent. Although possessing records of both the assembly and breakup of Rodinia, the position of the SCB in Rodinia remains debated, mainly due to disagreement on the tectonic settings of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism along the orogen. The magmatism has been interpreted either as subduction-related, placing South China on the margin of Rodinia, or as plume-related, suggesting a central position linking Australia and Laurentia. In this study, we investigate a gabbro-peridotite complex in the central Sibao orogen, with the peridotite showing cumulate textures and the gabbro giving a zircon U Pb crystallization age of 812.8 ± 5.7 Ma. These samples, together with a compilation of 560 published mafic–ultramafic and granitic samples within the 827 ± 22 Ma episode from the Sibao orogen, define cumulate lines of descent characterized by a continuous decline in Mg# with increasing SiO 2 , rather than the Z -shaped Mg#-SiO2 trend diagnostic of hydrous, subduction-related magmas, which is consistent with crystallization from anhydrous melts. This indicates that the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism originated from a dry mantle source, inconsistent with an active subduction setting. Mantle potential temperatures estimated from near-primary high-MgO basalts (MgO up to 17.5 wt%, Mg# up to 75, showing equilibrium with mantle) within this episode exceed 1525 °C, significantly above the ambient mantle potential temperature expected at ~827 Ma (1350–1420 °C), pointing to an anomalously hot mantle source. Initial 187 Os/ 188 Os isotopes of the studied cumulative peridotites (0.1376–0.1642) and coeval mafic–ultramafic rocks (0.1100–0.4191) indicate assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) processes involving asthenospheric components (plume), continental lithospheric mantle, and crustal material. This is consistent with plume underplating and interaction with the pre-existing subduction-modified lithosphere and overlying crust. Coeval plume-related magmatism is also documented elsewhere within the South China Block beyond the Sibao orogen, e.g., the Bikou flood basalts, further supporting a regional-scale thermal anomaly. Collectively, these observations are best explained by mantle plume activity, supporting a central position for the South China Block in Rodinia.

item.title: Fluid transport and reaction during disseminated mineralization in orogenic gold systems
item.dc:creator: Peng-Cong Zhang
Hao-Cheng Yu
Luca Smeraglia
Yong-Bao Gao
Chao Li
Jia-Nan Fu
Zhao-Hua Bai
Rui Wang
Kun-Feng Qiu
item.description: Disseminated mineralization is responsible for much of the production from orogenic gold deposits, and deciphering the nature of reactive fluid flow across the macro- to microscopic scale is crucial for understanding the mineralization processes. We integrated structural analysis, microstructural observations, whole-rock geochemistry, and thermodynamic modeling at the Liba orogenic gold deposit in the West Qinling orogen, central China, aiming to unravel the behavior of fluid in slate-hosted disseminated mineralization. The presence of deformed slaty foliation and hydrothermal sericite S-C fabrics indicates that the east-west−striking orebodies are hosted within sinistral brittle-ductile shear zones, which act as deposit-scale fluid migration conduits. The spatial distribution of pyrite within the mineralized slates closely aligns with pore networks and grain boundaries, suggesting that grain-scale enhanced permeability and fluid flow during mineralization were primarily accommodated by microcracking along grain boundaries. Combined with whole-rock geochemical data and thermodynamic modeling, we reveal that under conditions where rock permeability is sufficient for fluid transport, gold mineralization efficiency depends on the degree of reaction between the reactive fluid and Fe-bearing minerals. Compared to siliceous slate, the higher abundance of Fe-bearing minerals in argillaceous slate enhances reactivity at the fluid-mineral interface, promoting sulfide formation and gold precipitation. This study demonstrates that the physicochemical interplay between structural deformation and geochemical reactivity fundamentally controls disseminated mineralization in slate-hosted orogenic gold systems. Published: 2026-03-09T18:23:49+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Peng-Cong Zhang; Hao-Cheng Yu; Luca Smeraglia; Yong-Bao Gao; Chao Li; Jia-Nan Fu; Zhao-Hua Bai; Rui Wang; Kun-Feng Qiu

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38735.1

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Disseminated mineralization is responsible for much of the production from orogenic gold deposits, and deciphering the nature of reactive fluid flow across the macro- to microscopic scale is crucial for understanding the mineralization processes. We integrated structural analysis, microstructural observations, whole-rock geochemistry, and thermodynamic modeling at the Liba orogenic gold deposit in the West Qinling orogen, central China, aiming to unravel the behavior of fluid in slate-hosted disseminated mineralization. The presence of deformed slaty foliation and hydrothermal sericite S-C fabrics indicates that the east-west−striking orebodies are hosted within sinistral brittle-ductile shear zones, which act as deposit-scale fluid migration conduits. The spatial distribution of pyrite within the mineralized slates closely aligns with pore networks and grain boundaries, suggesting that grain-scale enhanced permeability and fluid flow during mineralization were primarily accommodated by microcracking along grain boundaries. Combined with whole-rock geochemical data and thermodynamic modeling, we reveal that under conditions where rock permeability is sufficient for fluid transport, gold mineralization efficiency depends on the degree of reaction between the reactive fluid and Fe-bearing minerals. Compared to siliceous slate, the higher abundance of Fe-bearing minerals in argillaceous slate enhances reactivity at the fluid-mineral interface, promoting sulfide formation and gold precipitation. This study demonstrates that the physicochemical interplay between structural deformation and geochemical reactivity fundamentally controls disseminated mineralization in slate-hosted orogenic gold systems.

item.title: Interaction between ilmenite-bearing cumulates and lunar mantle: implications for the origin of intermediate and high-titanium lunar basalts
item.dc:creator: Himela Moitra
Sujoy Ghosh
Tamalkanti Mukherjee
Saibal Gupta
Kuljeet Kaur Marhas
item.description: High-pressure layered and mixed experiments were performed at pressures of 1–3 GPa and temperatures of 1075–1500 °C in a piston cylinder apparatus to study the interaction between a novel (fayalite-rich) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) composition and ambient lunar mantle (represented by San Carlos olivine with X Mg of 0.91), to investigate the origin of intermediate- and high-Ti mare basalts (6–18 wt% TiO 2 ). The partial melts generated in the layered experiments show TiO 2 contents (9–19 wt%) similar to high-Ti mare basalts, but low MgO ( 2 ) of the IBC layer can directly fractionate to generate intermediate-Ti lunar basalts. Lower-temperature, high-Ti partial melts (11–19 wt% TiO 2 ) may first fractionate to more Ti-rich, Mg-poor compositions, then mix with ascending low-Ti, picritic magmas and undergo subsequent fractionation during ascent to generate high-Ti basalt compositions. Our model successfully reproduces the observed evolution of MgO, TiO 2 , SiO 2 , and FeO in high-Ti basalts, although it underestimates their Al 2 O 3 and CaO contents. Incorporation of pockets of plagioclase-rich melts from the mantle may resolve this discrepancy. High-pressure density calculations suggest that at 1 GPa, high-Ti partial melts can ascend to the lunar surface. At 2–3 GPa, some partial melts may ascend to the surface after fractionation and assimilation, while some may sink through the lunar mantle. These findings provide new insights on the role of lunar mantle overturn, IBC melting and mantle interactions in the formation of lunar high-Ti basalts and offer a viable mechanism for their generation throughout the history of mare volcanism. Published: 2026-03-11T00:21:33+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Himela Moitra; Sujoy Ghosh; Tamalkanti Mukherjee; Saibal Gupta; Kuljeet Kaur Marhas

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.008

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

High-pressure layered and mixed experiments were performed at pressures of 1–3 GPa and temperatures of 1075–1500 °C in a piston cylinder apparatus to study the interaction between a novel (fayalite-rich) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) composition and ambient lunar mantle (represented by San Carlos olivine with X Mg of 0.91), to investigate the origin of intermediate- and high-Ti mare basalts (6–18 wt% TiO 2 ). The partial melts generated in the layered experiments show TiO 2 contents (9–19 wt%) similar to high-Ti mare basalts, but low MgO ( 2 ) of the IBC layer can directly fractionate to generate intermediate-Ti lunar basalts. Lower-temperature, high-Ti partial melts (11–19 wt% TiO 2 ) may first fractionate to more Ti-rich, Mg-poor compositions, then mix with ascending low-Ti, picritic magmas and undergo subsequent fractionation during ascent to generate high-Ti basalt compositions. Our model successfully reproduces the observed evolution of MgO, TiO 2 , SiO 2 , and FeO in high-Ti basalts, although it underestimates their Al 2 O 3 and CaO contents. Incorporation of pockets of plagioclase-rich melts from the mantle may resolve this discrepancy. High-pressure density calculations suggest that at 1 GPa, high-Ti partial melts can ascend to the lunar surface. At 2–3 GPa, some partial melts may ascend to the surface after fractionation and assimilation, while some may sink through the lunar mantle. These findings provide new insights on the role of lunar mantle overturn, IBC melting and mantle interactions in the formation of lunar high-Ti basalts and offer a viable mechanism for their generation throughout the history of mare volcanism.

item.title: Diffusive copper redistribution from shallow degassed magmas to mafic enclaves. Insight from anomalously Cu-enriched enclaves from Bezymianny volcano, Kamchatka
item.dc:creator: Vesta O. Davydova
Vasily D. Shcherbakov
Pavel Yu. Plechov
Vasily O. Yapaskurt
Yuri D. Shcherbakov
Alexander B. Perepelov
Nikolay V. Brianskii
Viktor S. Antipin
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research
item.description: Mafic enclaves enriched in sulfides and copper compared to their host magmas are found in felsic plutons associated with porphyry deposits and in eruptive products of arc volcanoes. The origin of these Cu-rich enclaves is usually linked to either intrinsically Cu-rich parental magmas or to extensive fractionation of primitive magmas under sulfide-undersaturated conditions before they are stored in shallow felsic reservoirs. Using Bezymianny volcano as a case study, we suggest an alternative mechanism for the formation of Cu-rich mafic enclaves. Detailed petrographic analysis shows that sulfides in Bezymianny enclaves evolve from scarce early Cu-poor MSS (chilled monosulfide solid solution close to pyrrhotite or euhedral pyrrhotite with thin ISS rims) enclosed in phenocrysts, to Cu-rich ISS (intermediate solid solution close to cubanite) in matrix glass and vesicles. This increase in Cu content with crystallization requires the external addition of Cu. Mineral oxybarometry indicates that the shallow-stored magma (<1 kbar) had an oxygen fugacity of 0 to +1 log units above NNO. Intense degassing of S-bearing aqueous fluid kept sulfur concentrations in silicate melts below sulfide saturation, destabilizing sulfides and causing incompatible behavior of chalcophile elements. Consequently, Cu accumulated in silicate melt until inputs of sulfide-bearing mafic magma from deeper levels (9–2 kbar) triggered its diffusive redistribution into enclaves. Cu diffused from the Cu-rich, sulfide-free silicate melt of the shallow reservoir into the Cu-poor silicate melt of mafic enclaves, and subsequently into their sulfide phases. High diffusion rates of monovalent Cu allow small enclaves to achieve full Cu enrichment within several weeks. Published: 2026-03-09T16:47:29+00:00 Venue: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research Score: 0.575 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vesta O. Davydova; Vasily D. Shcherbakov; Pavel Yu. Plechov; Vasily O. Yapaskurt; Yuri D. Shcherbakov; Alexander B. Perepelov; Nikolay V. Brianskii; Viktor S. Antipin

Journal: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.jvolgeores.2026.108588

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Mafic enclaves enriched in sulfides and copper compared to their host magmas are found in felsic plutons associated with porphyry deposits and in eruptive products of arc volcanoes. The origin of these Cu-rich enclaves is usually linked to either intrinsically Cu-rich parental magmas or to extensive fractionation of primitive magmas under sulfide-undersaturated conditions before they are stored in shallow felsic reservoirs. Using Bezymianny volcano as a case study, we suggest an alternative mechanism for the formation of Cu-rich mafic enclaves. Detailed petrographic analysis shows that sulfides in Bezymianny enclaves evolve from scarce early Cu-poor MSS (chilled monosulfide solid solution close to pyrrhotite or euhedral pyrrhotite with thin ISS rims) enclosed in phenocrysts, to Cu-rich ISS (intermediate solid solution close to cubanite) in matrix glass and vesicles. This increase in Cu content with crystallization requires the external addition of Cu. Mineral oxybarometry indicates that the shallow-stored magma (<1 kbar) had an oxygen fugacity of 0 to +1 log units above NNO. Intense degassing of S-bearing aqueous fluid kept sulfur concentrations in silicate melts below sulfide saturation, destabilizing sulfides and causing incompatible behavior of chalcophile elements. Consequently, Cu accumulated in silicate melt until inputs of sulfide-bearing mafic magma from deeper levels (9–2 kbar) triggered its diffusive redistribution into enclaves. Cu diffused from the Cu-rich, sulfide-free silicate melt of the shallow reservoir into the Cu-poor silicate melt of mafic enclaves, and subsequently into their sulfide phases. High diffusion rates of monovalent Cu allow small enclaves to achieve full Cu enrichment within several weeks.

item.title: 3‐D Modeling of Differential Exhumation of Ultrahigh‐Pressure Metamorphic Rocks Driven by Increasing Plate Divergence
item.dc:creator: Xinxin Wang
Liang Zhao
Marco G. Malusà
Weiqiang Ji
Jie Yuan
item.description: Abstract Petrological and seismic constraints suggest differential sampling depth for ultrahigh pressure (UHP) metamorphic rocks exposed in different segments of the fossil subduction zone of the Western Alps. However, the mechanisms for the observed differential exhumation remain to be understood. Here, we account for the continental margin subduction within double subduction systems coupled with three‐dimensional (3‐D) geodynamic models to investigate the potential for this phenomenon. We tested two end‐member scenarios of upper‐plate motion, including Adria counterclockwise rotation and divergent motion away from the trench. Results show that both scenarios can produce a unidirectional increase in the metamorphic peak of exhumed UHP rocks along the orogen strike. Only in the simulations where the counterclockwise rotation of the upper plate acts as the primary driver of exhumation did the resulting characteristics of the deep structure fit with those observed in the Western Alps. They include the presence of an exhumed mantle wedge beneath the southern UHP domes and its absence beneath the northern UHP domes. Our results can be exported to other subduction zones where kinematic constraints suggest a component of upper‐plate rotation, such as eastern Papua New Guinea, and to older subduction zones where the kinematics is poorly assessed. Published: 2026-03-08T09:16:43+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.575 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xinxin Wang; Liang Zhao; Marco G. Malusà; Weiqiang Ji; Jie Yuan

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012683

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Abstract Petrological and seismic constraints suggest differential sampling depth for ultrahigh pressure (UHP) metamorphic rocks exposed in different segments of the fossil subduction zone of the Western Alps. However, the mechanisms for the observed differential exhumation remain to be understood. Here, we account for the continental margin subduction within double subduction systems coupled with three‐dimensional (3‐D) geodynamic models to investigate the potential for this phenomenon. We tested two end‐member scenarios of upper‐plate motion, including Adria counterclockwise rotation and divergent motion away from the trench. Results show that both scenarios can produce a unidirectional increase in the metamorphic peak of exhumed UHP rocks along the orogen strike. Only in the simulations where the counterclockwise rotation of the upper plate acts as the primary driver of exhumation did the resulting characteristics of the deep structure fit with those observed in the Western Alps. They include the presence of an exhumed mantle wedge beneath the southern UHP domes and its absence beneath the northern UHP domes. Our results can be exported to other subduction zones where kinematic constraints suggest a component of upper‐plate rotation, such as eastern Papua New Guinea, and to older subduction zones where the kinematics is poorly assessed.

item.title: The lithospheric carbonatite pathway at Bayan Obo: Evidence from gravity and magnetic inversion and its significance for Nb-REE-Fe mineralization
item.dc:creator: Yongkang Chan
Wenna Zhou
Bohu Xu
Jiaqi Dong
Sihati A
Qingyan Tang
item.description: Clarifying the geometry of deep carbonatite-fluid pathways beneath the supergiant Bayan Obo Nb-REE-Fe deposit, and their coupling with mineralization can provide key constraints for establishing models of ore-forming evolution and for predicting deep mineralization. In this study, satellite gravity data and aeromagnetic data are used to invert the lithospheric-scale density and magnetic susceptibility structures of the Bayan Obo region. The results show that a southward-dipping pathway system rising from the mantle is identified beneath the ore district. The results are in agreement with previously published magnetotelluric inversion profiles. Indication by multiple methods in combination that this system is characterized by the coincidence of high-density, high-magnetic-susceptibility, and low-resistivity anomalies. Curie depth estimates reveal pronounced Curie surface uplifts in the ore district and its surrounding areas. Together with the high-density, high-magnetic-susceptibility, low-resistivity anomaly belt, these features indicate a persistent deep thermal anomaly. In combination with existing constraints from rock physical property measurements, mineralogical observations, petrological geochemical and geochronological data, this study suggests that Nb-REE-Fe mineralization at Bayan Obo is controlled by a deep carbonatite pathway system. This system was initiated during Mesoproterozoic rifting and was repeatedly reactivated during Mesoproterozoic magmatism (∼1.3 Ga), Early Paleozoic fluid activity (∼0.44 Ga), and Permian granitic magmatism (∼0.27 Ga). This study provides new geophysical evidence for finely characterizing the geometry of deep carbonatite pathways beneath Bayan Obo and their control on multi-stage Nb-REE-Fe mineralization, and offers a reference for deep exploration of similar carbonatite-type Nb-REE-Fe deposits. Published: 2026-03-11T07:45:20+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.575 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yongkang Chan; Wenna Zhou; Bohu Xu; Jiaqi Dong; Sihati A; Qingyan Tang

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108033

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Clarifying the geometry of deep carbonatite-fluid pathways beneath the supergiant Bayan Obo Nb-REE-Fe deposit, and their coupling with mineralization can provide key constraints for establishing models of ore-forming evolution and for predicting deep mineralization. In this study, satellite gravity data and aeromagnetic data are used to invert the lithospheric-scale density and magnetic susceptibility structures of the Bayan Obo region. The results show that a southward-dipping pathway system rising from the mantle is identified beneath the ore district. The results are in agreement with previously published magnetotelluric inversion profiles. Indication by multiple methods in combination that this system is characterized by the coincidence of high-density, high-magnetic-susceptibility, and low-resistivity anomalies. Curie depth estimates reveal pronounced Curie surface uplifts in the ore district and its surrounding areas. Together with the high-density, high-magnetic-susceptibility, low-resistivity anomaly belt, these features indicate a persistent deep thermal anomaly. In combination with existing constraints from rock physical property measurements, mineralogical observations, petrological geochemical and geochronological data, this study suggests that Nb-REE-Fe mineralization at Bayan Obo is controlled by a deep carbonatite pathway system. This system was initiated during Mesoproterozoic rifting and was repeatedly reactivated during Mesoproterozoic magmatism (∼1.3 Ga), Early Paleozoic fluid activity (∼0.44 Ga), and Permian granitic magmatism (∼0.27 Ga). This study provides new geophysical evidence for finely characterizing the geometry of deep carbonatite pathways beneath Bayan Obo and their control on multi-stage Nb-REE-Fe mineralization, and offers a reference for deep exploration of similar carbonatite-type Nb-REE-Fe deposits.

item.title: Basin-scale sulfur cycling driven by volcanism in Southern high latitudes during the Oceanic Anoxic Event 2
item.dc:creator: Zhiyu Zeng
Yilun Xu
Weiqi Yao
item.description: The Oceanic Anoxic Event 2 (OAE2) represents the episode of large-scale ocean oxygen loss under extreme warmth in the late Cretaceous. The initiation of the OAE2 has been attributed to massive volcanic carbon emissions related to the emplacement of Large Igneous Provinces (LIPs), accompanied by significant perturbations in biogeochemical cycles of carbon, oxygen, and sulfur. Given the lower sulfate concentration in the Cretaceous ocean than it is today, its residence time was shorter under comparable fluxes, increasing the likelihood of spatial heterogeneity in the marine sulfur cycle. Since most existing data of seawater sulfate S-isotope ratio (δ 34 S) for the OAE2 are reported from localities distant from LIPs, our understanding of the effects of volcanism on regional sulfur cycling and climate warming remains poorly constrained. In this study, we present marine barite δ 34 S and TEX 86 -derived sea-surface temperature (SST) records across the OAE2 from the Mentelle Basin, which was proximal to the active Kerguelen LIP (KLIP) in Southern high latitudes. Our result shows a basin-scale ∼5‰ decrease in seawater sulfate δ 34 S during the OAE2, followed by a recovery to the higher-than-pre-excursion value in the post-OAE2. The simple box model attributes the δ 34 S decline to a volcanic pulse of 6.96 × 10 12 mol isotopically light sulfur released into the ocean within 50 thousand years, which is approximately threefold of the background flux. The subsequent δ 34 S overshoot requires a coeval increase in pyrite burial flux by 10%, indicating a modest expansion of anoxia in the Mentelle Basin. Meanwhile, the TEX 86 -derived SST documents prolonged warmth persisting into the post-OAE2, providing an independent constraint on thermal forcing during the recovery phase. Sustained elevated SST in the high-latitude Southern Hemisphere would not only have promoted basin-scale deoxygenation and pyrite burial beyond the initial volcanic perturbation, but also reduced the meridional SST gradient and efficiency of poleward heat redistribution, thereby acting as positive feedback that helped sustain global warming. These findings reveal the response of basin-scale sulfur cycling and persistent warmth to volcanic activities in the high-latitude ocean across the OAE2. Published: 2026-03-14T16:03:34+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.573 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhiyu Zeng; Yilun Xu; Weiqi Yao

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.023

Relevance: 0.573 (consider)

The Oceanic Anoxic Event 2 (OAE2) represents the episode of large-scale ocean oxygen loss under extreme warmth in the late Cretaceous. The initiation of the OAE2 has been attributed to massive volcanic carbon emissions related to the emplacement of Large Igneous Provinces (LIPs), accompanied by significant perturbations in biogeochemical cycles of carbon, oxygen, and sulfur. Given the lower sulfate concentration in the Cretaceous ocean than it is today, its residence time was shorter under comparable fluxes, increasing the likelihood of spatial heterogeneity in the marine sulfur cycle. Since most existing data of seawater sulfate S-isotope ratio (δ 34 S) for the OAE2 are reported from localities distant from LIPs, our understanding of the effects of volcanism on regional sulfur cycling and climate warming remains poorly constrained. In this study, we present marine barite δ 34 S and TEX 86 -derived sea-surface temperature (SST) records across the OAE2 from the Mentelle Basin, which was proximal to the active Kerguelen LIP (KLIP) in Southern high latitudes. Our result shows a basin-scale ∼5‰ decrease in seawater sulfate δ 34 S during the OAE2, followed by a recovery to the higher-than-pre-excursion value in the post-OAE2. The simple box model attributes the δ 34 S decline to a volcanic pulse of 6.96 × 10 12 mol isotopically light sulfur released into the ocean within 50 thousand years, which is approximately threefold of the background flux. The subsequent δ 34 S overshoot requires a coeval increase in pyrite burial flux by 10%, indicating a modest expansion of anoxia in the Mentelle Basin. Meanwhile, the TEX 86 -derived SST documents prolonged warmth persisting into the post-OAE2, providing an independent constraint on thermal forcing during the recovery phase. Sustained elevated SST in the high-latitude Southern Hemisphere would not only have promoted basin-scale deoxygenation and pyrite burial beyond the initial volcanic perturbation, but also reduced the meridional SST gradient and efficiency of poleward heat redistribution, thereby acting as positive feedback that helped sustain global warming. These findings reveal the response of basin-scale sulfur cycling and persistent warmth to volcanic activities in the high-latitude ocean across the OAE2.

item.title: Astronomical calibration of the middle Cambrian in Baltica: global carbon cycle synchronization and climate dynamics
item.dc:creator: Valentin JAMART
Damien PAS
Linda A. HINNOV
Jorge E. SPANGENBERG
Thierry ADATTE
Arne T. NIELSEN
Niels H. SCHOVSBO
Nicolas THIBAULT
Michiel ARTS
Allison C. DALEY
item.description: Abstract The Alum Shale Formation of Baltica preserves one of the most continuous and fossil-rich records for the Cambrian Period. Thus, the Alum Shale is a key sedimentary archive for refining global chronostratigraphy, reconstructing carbon cycle perturbations, and assessing astronomical forcing of high-latitude systems during an early Palaeozoic greenhouse world. Here we present a high-resolution cyclostratigraphic and multiproxy study of the middle Cambrian succession from the Albjära-1 drill core (southern Sweden), anchored by a high-precision U–Pb age. Integration of our astronomical time scale with carbon isotope data and refined biostratigraphy places the Albjära-1 core as a global reference record. This framework provides numerically constrained ages and durations for the Drumian Carbon Isotope Excursion (DICE), enabling worldwide synchronization of biostratigraphy and carbon cycle events. Coupled elemental geochemistry and time calibration reveal that obliquity- and orbital eccentricity-driven climate oscillations modulated sea-level and dust fluxes, highlighting the sensitivity of Earth’s early Paleozoic greenhouse systems to astronomical forcing. Published: 2026-03-13T14:34:30+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.573 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Valentin JAMART; Damien PAS; Linda A. HINNOV; Jorge E. SPANGENBERG; Thierry ADATTE; Arne T. NIELSEN; Niels H. SCHOVSBO; Nicolas THIBAULT; Michiel ARTS; Allison C. DALEY

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-70651-5

Relevance: 0.573 (consider)

Abstract The Alum Shale Formation of Baltica preserves one of the most continuous and fossil-rich records for the Cambrian Period. Thus, the Alum Shale is a key sedimentary archive for refining global chronostratigraphy, reconstructing carbon cycle perturbations, and assessing astronomical forcing of high-latitude systems during an early Palaeozoic greenhouse world. Here we present a high-resolution cyclostratigraphic and multiproxy study of the middle Cambrian succession from the Albjära-1 drill core (southern Sweden), anchored by a high-precision U–Pb age. Integration of our astronomical time scale with carbon isotope data and refined biostratigraphy places the Albjära-1 core as a global reference record. This framework provides numerically constrained ages and durations for the Drumian Carbon Isotope Excursion (DICE), enabling worldwide synchronization of biostratigraphy and carbon cycle events. Coupled elemental geochemistry and time calibration reveal that obliquity- and orbital eccentricity-driven climate oscillations modulated sea-level and dust fluxes, highlighting the sensitivity of Earth’s early Paleozoic greenhouse systems to astronomical forcing.

item.title: The Paleo‐Tethys Suture Zone in the Afghan Hindu Kush‒Pamir: Geo‐Thermochronology, Geochemistry, Tectonics
item.dc:creator: Lothar Ratschbacher
Said Amin Hashimy
Hasan Malistani
Yasuhito Osanai
Jianhua Li
Zhao Yang
Bradley R. Hacker
Leander Franz
Hadi Karimi
item.description: Abstract In the Afghan Hindu Kush, the 223–209 Ma (≤800°C) Salang batholith is part of the Silk‐Road magmatic arc that was built on ∼40‐km‐thick Turan‐Karakum block continental crust. The batholith constitutes the hanging wall of the Herat‐Panjshir‐Badakhshan—the Paleo‐Tethys—suture zone, vestige of the subducted Paleozoic‐early Mesozoic Paleo‐Tethys oceanic lithosphere. It intruded Precambrian (≤∼610 Ma) paragneiss and Ordovician (∼480 Ma) orthogneiss. 208–180 Ma anorogenic leucogranites (≤700°C) along the suture formed by mica‐dehydration melting in ∼30‐km‐thick crust. Post‐emplacement cooling rates declined from ∼35 to 3°C/Myr. In the Afghan Pamir, 211–180 Ma anatectic leucogranites accompanied ≤750°C metamorphism in mostly Middle Triassic accretionary‐wedge sediments; this rock association cooled at ∼20°C/Myr. The wedge was built on 329–275 Ma continental‐arc and MORB‒island‐arc (IAB) rocks, and incorporated ocean‐island (OIB) and MORB rocks; their 1.02–1.15 Ga T (Nd) model ages indicate a significant contribution of juvenile crust, with one MORB ( ε Nd (i) = +8.59) recording a typical Paleo‐Tethys oceanic crust signature. Detrital zircons of the wedge rocks indicate both proximal and distal provenance. The magmatic rocks of the Afghan Pamir, NE Iran, and northwestern Tibet delineate a Carboniferous arc. The Salang‐batholith arc magmatism, the post‐collisional magmatism, and the related regional metamorphism suggest a ∼210 Ma closure of the Paleo‐Tethys ocean. A distinct plate configuration in the embayment between the Turan‐Karakum and Tarim blocks explains the proximity of sites with major accretion and back‐arc extension, weak accretion and accretionary‐wedge subduction associated with high‐grade metamorphism, and subduction erosion and wedge subduction along the Paleo‐Tethys suture in Afghanistan, Tajikistan, and western Tibet. Published: 2026-03-08T15:03:46+00:00 Venue: Tectonics Score: 0.563 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lothar Ratschbacher; Said Amin Hashimy; Hasan Malistani; Yasuhito Osanai; Jianhua Li; Zhao Yang; Bradley R. Hacker; Leander Franz; Hadi Karimi

Journal: Tectonics

DOI: 10.1029/2025tc009220

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

Abstract In the Afghan Hindu Kush, the 223–209 Ma (≤800°C) Salang batholith is part of the Silk‐Road magmatic arc that was built on ∼40‐km‐thick Turan‐Karakum block continental crust. The batholith constitutes the hanging wall of the Herat‐Panjshir‐Badakhshan—the Paleo‐Tethys—suture zone, vestige of the subducted Paleozoic‐early Mesozoic Paleo‐Tethys oceanic lithosphere. It intruded Precambrian (≤∼610 Ma) paragneiss and Ordovician (∼480 Ma) orthogneiss. 208–180 Ma anorogenic leucogranites (≤700°C) along the suture formed by mica‐dehydration melting in ∼30‐km‐thick crust. Post‐emplacement cooling rates declined from ∼35 to 3°C/Myr. In the Afghan Pamir, 211–180 Ma anatectic leucogranites accompanied ≤750°C metamorphism in mostly Middle Triassic accretionary‐wedge sediments; this rock association cooled at ∼20°C/Myr. The wedge was built on 329–275 Ma continental‐arc and MORB‒island‐arc (IAB) rocks, and incorporated ocean‐island (OIB) and MORB rocks; their 1.02–1.15 Ga T (Nd) model ages indicate a significant contribution of juvenile crust, with one MORB ( ε Nd (i) = +8.59) recording a typical Paleo‐Tethys oceanic crust signature. Detrital zircons of the wedge rocks indicate both proximal and distal provenance. The magmatic rocks of the Afghan Pamir, NE Iran, and northwestern Tibet delineate a Carboniferous arc. The Salang‐batholith arc magmatism, the post‐collisional magmatism, and the related regional metamorphism suggest a ∼210 Ma closure of the Paleo‐Tethys ocean. A distinct plate configuration in the embayment between the Turan‐Karakum and Tarim blocks explains the proximity of sites with major accretion and back‐arc extension, weak accretion and accretionary‐wedge subduction associated with high‐grade metamorphism, and subduction erosion and wedge subduction along the Paleo‐Tethys suture in Afghanistan, Tajikistan, and western Tibet.

item.title: Slowly Migrating Fracture Swarms in an Actively Serpentinizing Borehole
item.dc:creator: John M. Aiken
Fabian Barras
François Renard
Greg Hirth
Peter B. Kelemen
Robert A. Sohn
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Peridotite rocks are primary targets for engineered geological carbon sequestration efforts because the carbon in ‐bearing fluids is transferred to the rock in the form of carbonate minerals during alteration reactions. Sequestration efforts must necessarily open fractures in the rocks surrounding a pumped borehole, but the current understanding of fracture growth during serpentinization of peridotite is limited to theoretical models and laboratory experiments on small samples. We deployed hydrophone arrays in peridotite boreholes established by the Oman Drilling Program and detected downward migrating fracture swarms that represent the first field observations of fracture growth in a serpentinizing rock. More than 2 years after the boreholes were established, we detected four, downward migrating tensile fracture swarms during an interval of elevated pore pressure following large rain events. All of the swarms occurred within a partially confined section of the local aquifer, beginning at a depth of 170 m and migrating to the bottom of the 400 m‐deep hole at average rates of 6–20 cm.. We demonstrate that pore fluid processes can explain both the triggering of the tensile fracture swarms and their slow migration rates. Our results indicate that the tip of fractures propagating away from the borehole maintained near‐critical states over time such that relatively small increases in fluid pressure triggered tensile fracturing episodes, indicating that carbon sequestration efforts in mafic rocks should be able to open fractures and expose fresh rock to mineralization. Published: 2026-03-08T10:56:33+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.563 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: John M. Aiken; Fabian Barras; François Renard; Greg Hirth; Peter B. Kelemen; Robert A. Sohn

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb032133

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

Abstract Peridotite rocks are primary targets for engineered geological carbon sequestration efforts because the carbon in ‐bearing fluids is transferred to the rock in the form of carbonate minerals during alteration reactions. Sequestration efforts must necessarily open fractures in the rocks surrounding a pumped borehole, but the current understanding of fracture growth during serpentinization of peridotite is limited to theoretical models and laboratory experiments on small samples. We deployed hydrophone arrays in peridotite boreholes established by the Oman Drilling Program and detected downward migrating fracture swarms that represent the first field observations of fracture growth in a serpentinizing rock. More than 2 years after the boreholes were established, we detected four, downward migrating tensile fracture swarms during an interval of elevated pore pressure following large rain events. All of the swarms occurred within a partially confined section of the local aquifer, beginning at a depth of 170 m and migrating to the bottom of the 400 m‐deep hole at average rates of 6–20 cm.. We demonstrate that pore fluid processes can explain both the triggering of the tensile fracture swarms and their slow migration rates. Our results indicate that the tip of fractures propagating away from the borehole maintained near‐critical states over time such that relatively small increases in fluid pressure triggered tensile fracturing episodes, indicating that carbon sequestration efforts in mafic rocks should be able to open fractures and expose fresh rock to mineralization.

item.title: Two‐Stage Dolomite Formation in Carbonate Platforms Revealed by Carbonate Clumped Isotope Thermometry
item.dc:creator: Y. Levenson
J. M. Eiler
E. Wurgaft
N. Neagu
Y. Ebert
U. Ryb
item.description: Abstract The “dolomite problem” refers to the scarcity of dolomite in Cenozoic marine environments compared with its abundance in earlier strata. This discrepancy has been attributed to changes in marine environments or to insufficient thermal maturity required for dolomite formation. We measured carbon, oxygen, and clumped isotope compositions of carbonate rocks from the Albian Nahal Me'arot reef complex (north Israel), where dolomite is confined to back‐reef lagoon facies and associated with reflux‐brine dolomitization, a process thought responsible for large volumes of dolomite in Phanerozoic rocks. Results show a negative trend between TΔ 47 (29–54°C) and δ 18 O dolomite (−3.25 to −1.35 ‰VPDB), consistent with burial alteration, and support a two‐stage process: (a) proto‐dolomite formation near the lagoon floor, and (b) recrystallization during burial. Together with compilations of Mg/Ca ratios in Cenozoic marine carbonates predicting more dolomite than observed directly, our results suggest that insufficient thermal maturity has limited dolomite formation in Cenozoic strata. Published: 2026-03-09T04:12:24+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.559 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Y. Levenson; J. M. Eiler; E. Wurgaft; N. Neagu; Y. Ebert; U. Ryb

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl120386

Relevance: 0.559 (consider)

Abstract The “dolomite problem” refers to the scarcity of dolomite in Cenozoic marine environments compared with its abundance in earlier strata. This discrepancy has been attributed to changes in marine environments or to insufficient thermal maturity required for dolomite formation. We measured carbon, oxygen, and clumped isotope compositions of carbonate rocks from the Albian Nahal Me'arot reef complex (north Israel), where dolomite is confined to back‐reef lagoon facies and associated with reflux‐brine dolomitization, a process thought responsible for large volumes of dolomite in Phanerozoic rocks. Results show a negative trend between TΔ 47 (29–54°C) and δ 18 O dolomite (−3.25 to −1.35 ‰VPDB), consistent with burial alteration, and support a two‐stage process: (a) proto‐dolomite formation near the lagoon floor, and (b) recrystallization during burial. Together with compilations of Mg/Ca ratios in Cenozoic marine carbonates predicting more dolomite than observed directly, our results suggest that insufficient thermal maturity has limited dolomite formation in Cenozoic strata.

item.title: Tracking high-field-strength-element enrichment pathways in alkaline granites: Insights from potassium and barium isotopes
item.dc:creator: Hao-Cheng Yu
Zheng-Yu Long
A.E. Williams-Jones
Ralf Halama
Frédéric Moynier
Shan-Shan Li
Ming-Qian Wu
Hao-Ran Duan
Jia-Dong Ma
Nan Ju
Ze-Zhou Wang
Kun-Feng Qiu
item.description: High-field-strength-element (HFSE)-enriched alkaline granites serve as critical repositories for metals such as Zr, Nb, and the rare earth elements (REEs). However, the extent to which HFSE enrichment in alkaline granites reflects magmatic differentiation versus hydrothermal overprint remains debated. Here, we employ potassium (K) and barium (Ba) isotope systematics from the Baerzhe peralkaline granite pluton in NE China, which hosts a world-class Zr-Nb-Be-REE deposit, to distinguish the respective roles of magmatic and hydrothermal processes in HFSE enrichment. Whole-rock major and trace element determinations, Nd isotope measurements, and high-precision K and Ba isotope analyses were conducted on barren and ore-related granite samples. The ore-related granites are markedly Ba-depleted (8.02–42.1 ppm) with highly variable δ 138/134 Ba (−0.35 to + 0.15 ‰), whereas the barren granites retain high Ba contents (910–1454 ppm) and homogeneous δ 138/134 Ba (−0.05 to +0.04 ‰). Likewise, ore-related samples record a broader range of isotopically lower δ 41 K values (down to − 0.62 ‰), in contrast to the narrow, near-crustal δ 41 K range of barren granites (–0.49 to –0.35 ‰). These geochemical and isotopic disparities reflect distinct evolutionary paths. Our results reveal that extensive fractional crystallization plays a pivotal role in HFSE pre-concentration. Prolonged crystallization of alkali feldspar preferentially sequesters heavy K isotopes, yielding an isotopically lighter residual melt that formed the HFSE-rich granites. Barren granites, with limited feldspar removal, preserve crust-like δ 41 K signatures. In contrast, Ba isotopes are highly sensitive to post-magmatic fluids. Instead of trending towards heavier Ba isotope compositions under K-feldspar-dominated closed-system fractional crystallization, the Baerzhe ore-related granites show lower and more variable δ 138/134 Ba due to late-stage Na- and F-rich hydrothermal metasomatism that remobilized HFSEs and induced Ba isotopic exchange. This is reflected by the correlation between δ 138/134 Ba values and the concentrations of these elements. Notably, this fluid overprint altered Ba isotopic compositions without measurably shifting δ 41 K, which remained governed by its magmatic history. Our study demonstrates that K isotopes reliably record magmatic differentiation, whereas Ba isotopes serve as sensitive monitors of subsequent hydrothermal processes. The complementary use of these isotopic systems provides a robust framework for disentangling the complex interplay between magmatic and hydrothermal processes in alkaline granite-hosted HFSE ore systems. Published: 2026-03-12T16:55:29+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.557 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hao-Cheng Yu; Zheng-Yu Long; A.E. Williams-Jones; Ralf Halama; Frédéric Moynier; Shan-Shan Li; Ming-Qian Wu; Hao-Ran Duan; Jia-Dong Ma; Nan Ju; Ze-Zhou Wang; Kun-Feng Qiu

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.017

Relevance: 0.557 (consider)

High-field-strength-element (HFSE)-enriched alkaline granites serve as critical repositories for metals such as Zr, Nb, and the rare earth elements (REEs). However, the extent to which HFSE enrichment in alkaline granites reflects magmatic differentiation versus hydrothermal overprint remains debated. Here, we employ potassium (K) and barium (Ba) isotope systematics from the Baerzhe peralkaline granite pluton in NE China, which hosts a world-class Zr-Nb-Be-REE deposit, to distinguish the respective roles of magmatic and hydrothermal processes in HFSE enrichment. Whole-rock major and trace element determinations, Nd isotope measurements, and high-precision K and Ba isotope analyses were conducted on barren and ore-related granite samples. The ore-related granites are markedly Ba-depleted (8.02–42.1 ppm) with highly variable δ 138/134 Ba (−0.35 to + 0.15 ‰), whereas the barren granites retain high Ba contents (910–1454 ppm) and homogeneous δ 138/134 Ba (−0.05 to +0.04 ‰). Likewise, ore-related samples record a broader range of isotopically lower δ 41 K values (down to − 0.62 ‰), in contrast to the narrow, near-crustal δ 41 K range of barren granites (–0.49 to –0.35 ‰). These geochemical and isotopic disparities reflect distinct evolutionary paths. Our results reveal that extensive fractional crystallization plays a pivotal role in HFSE pre-concentration. Prolonged crystallization of alkali feldspar preferentially sequesters heavy K isotopes, yielding an isotopically lighter residual melt that formed the HFSE-rich granites. Barren granites, with limited feldspar removal, preserve crust-like δ 41 K signatures. In contrast, Ba isotopes are highly sensitive to post-magmatic fluids. Instead of trending towards heavier Ba isotope compositions under K-feldspar-dominated closed-system fractional crystallization, the Baerzhe ore-related granites show lower and more variable δ 138/134 Ba due to late-stage Na- and F-rich hydrothermal metasomatism that remobilized HFSEs and induced Ba isotopic exchange. This is reflected by the correlation between δ 138/134 Ba values and the concentrations of these elements. Notably, this fluid overprint altered Ba isotopic compositions without measurably shifting δ 41 K, which remained governed by its magmatic history. Our study demonstrates that K isotopes reliably record magmatic differentiation, whereas Ba isotopes serve as sensitive monitors of subsequent hydrothermal processes. The complementary use of these isotopic systems provides a robust framework for disentangling the complex interplay between magmatic and hydrothermal processes in alkaline granite-hosted HFSE ore systems.

item.title: Extensive thallium mineralization resulting from diagenetic iron sulfide adsorption preceding MVT Zn-Pb mineralization at Jinding, Southwestern China
item.dc:creator: Hongyu Duan
Qingfei Wang
Jun Deng
Jiaxuan Zhu
Weihong Ma
item.description: The Jinding deposit in the Lanping Basin, southwestern China, is a world-class Mississippi Valley-type (MVT) Zn–Pb deposit hosted within a salt diapir dome and contains significant Tl resources (~ 8167 t). Integrated field, mineralogical, and mineral chemical studies reveal a rare type of Tl mineralization hosted by diagenetic iron sulfides in the deposit. Five types of pyrite (Py) and marcasite (Mar) of spheroid Py0 nuclei, epitaxial zonal PyA, plumose MarA, granular PyB and MarB are identified with variable Tl concentration significantly higher than that in MVT sphalerite (Sp) of disseminated SpA, colloform SpB, and crystalline SpC. The trace element signatures, combined with highly negative and variable δ34S values (–34.00‰ to + 1.41‰ (VCDT)) indicate a low-temperature, shallow, diagenetic origin of these iron sulfides. Mass balance effect accompanied with great increase in volume abundance from early- to late-stage iron sulfides controls the decreasing trend of Tl concentration across paragenetic sequences. Tl incorporation into iron sulfides is controlled by As-dependent solid-state solubility under high-As fluid conditions, whereas an upper concentration limit of ~ 1000 ppm governs Tl incorporation under low-As fluid conditions. Fluid pH likely plays an important role in controlling the differential Tl enrichment in closely associated PyB and MarB, as well as in different host rocks. The spatial and temporal disparity between Tl-rich iron sulfides and Zn-Pb sulfides, as well as Tl deficiency in MVT sphalerite indicate that Tl precipitated early in a distinct mineralization event preceding MVT Zn-Pb mineralization. Published: 2026-03-12T08:23:44+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.557 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hongyu Duan; Qingfei Wang; Jun Deng; Jiaxuan Zhu; Weihong Ma

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01439-2

Relevance: 0.557 (consider)

The Jinding deposit in the Lanping Basin, southwestern China, is a world-class Mississippi Valley-type (MVT) Zn–Pb deposit hosted within a salt diapir dome and contains significant Tl resources (~ 8167 t). Integrated field, mineralogical, and mineral chemical studies reveal a rare type of Tl mineralization hosted by diagenetic iron sulfides in the deposit. Five types of pyrite (Py) and marcasite (Mar) of spheroid Py0 nuclei, epitaxial zonal PyA, plumose MarA, granular PyB and MarB are identified with variable Tl concentration significantly higher than that in MVT sphalerite (Sp) of disseminated SpA, colloform SpB, and crystalline SpC. The trace element signatures, combined with highly negative and variable δ34S values (–34.00‰ to + 1.41‰ (VCDT)) indicate a low-temperature, shallow, diagenetic origin of these iron sulfides. Mass balance effect accompanied with great increase in volume abundance from early- to late-stage iron sulfides controls the decreasing trend of Tl concentration across paragenetic sequences. Tl incorporation into iron sulfides is controlled by As-dependent solid-state solubility under high-As fluid conditions, whereas an upper concentration limit of ~ 1000 ppm governs Tl incorporation under low-As fluid conditions. Fluid pH likely plays an important role in controlling the differential Tl enrichment in closely associated PyB and MarB, as well as in different host rocks. The spatial and temporal disparity between Tl-rich iron sulfides and Zn-Pb sulfides, as well as Tl deficiency in MVT sphalerite indicate that Tl precipitated early in a distinct mineralization event preceding MVT Zn-Pb mineralization.

item.title: Reliability of quartz-hosted melt inclusions and discrete rhyolite reservoirs revealed by H isotopes, trace elements, and volatiles
item.dc:creator: Sarah M. Hickernell
Ayla S. Pamukçu
Brian D. Monteleone
item.description: High-silica magmas (≥68 weight % silicon dioxide) have produced some of the largest and most impactful volcanic eruptions in Earth’s history. Quartz-hosted melt inclusions can provide critical insights into the architecture, evolution, and eruption of silicic magmas; however, the reliability of mineral-hosted melt inclusion compositions has been questioned because of potential postentrapment modification of inclusions via diffusive exchange. We demonstrate that with careful sample selection, both naturally glassy and experimentally homogenized quartz-hosted melt inclusions can retain meaningful preeruptive signatures and provide useful insights into preeruptive magmatic conditions. We also show that hydrogen isotope measurements and detailed descriptions of melt inclusion geometries are particularly critical for identifying postentrapment diffusive exchange in melt inclusions, whether it is due to natural or experimental processes. Last, combining data from measurements of quartz-hosted melt inclusion hydrogen isotopes, trace elements, volatiles, and geometries, we fingerprint multiple discrete rhyolite magma reservoirs in the Highland Range volcanic sequence (NV, USA). Published: 2026-03-13T17:58:33+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.557 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sarah M. Hickernell; Ayla S. Pamukçu; Brian D. Monteleone

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.adw1667

Relevance: 0.557 (consider)

High-silica magmas (≥68 weight % silicon dioxide) have produced some of the largest and most impactful volcanic eruptions in Earth’s history. Quartz-hosted melt inclusions can provide critical insights into the architecture, evolution, and eruption of silicic magmas; however, the reliability of mineral-hosted melt inclusion compositions has been questioned because of potential postentrapment modification of inclusions via diffusive exchange. We demonstrate that with careful sample selection, both naturally glassy and experimentally homogenized quartz-hosted melt inclusions can retain meaningful preeruptive signatures and provide useful insights into preeruptive magmatic conditions. We also show that hydrogen isotope measurements and detailed descriptions of melt inclusion geometries are particularly critical for identifying postentrapment diffusive exchange in melt inclusions, whether it is due to natural or experimental processes. Last, combining data from measurements of quartz-hosted melt inclusion hydrogen isotopes, trace elements, volatiles, and geometries, we fingerprint multiple discrete rhyolite magma reservoirs in the Highland Range volcanic sequence (NV, USA).

item.title: Volatile resorption expedites eruption onset in large silicic systems
item.dc:creator: Franziska Keller
Meredith Townsend
Juliana Troch
Christian Huber
item.description: Abstract Silicic caldera-forming eruptions are among the most hazardous natural phenomena on Earth, yet their triggering mechanisms remain poorly understood. While volatile exsolution is widely recognized as a potential eruption driver in large silicic systems, we find that volatile resorption can, counterintuitively, promote chamber pressurization faster than volatile exsolution. Using a thermo-mechanical magma chamber model, we show that resorption is a common process in rapidly recharged systems, driven by pressure increase and crystal melting. The Aso-4 eruption offers a natural case where volatile resorption may have occurred, with model results predicting resorption at recharge rates >10 -2.4 km 3 /yr. Through reducing bulk magma compressibility, resorption amplifies pressurization, driving chamber destabilization and potentially expediting eruption onset. Here, we propose that volatile resorption is a natural process both accommodating and promoting rapid chamber pressurization, fundamental to destabilizing large-scale silicic systems. Detecting its signatures in monitoring signals could provide early warning of imminent eruption. Published: 2026-03-12T23:04:14+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.553 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Franziska Keller; Meredith Townsend; Juliana Troch; Christian Huber

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-70206-8

Relevance: 0.553 (consider)

Abstract Silicic caldera-forming eruptions are among the most hazardous natural phenomena on Earth, yet their triggering mechanisms remain poorly understood. While volatile exsolution is widely recognized as a potential eruption driver in large silicic systems, we find that volatile resorption can, counterintuitively, promote chamber pressurization faster than volatile exsolution. Using a thermo-mechanical magma chamber model, we show that resorption is a common process in rapidly recharged systems, driven by pressure increase and crystal melting. The Aso-4 eruption offers a natural case where volatile resorption may have occurred, with model results predicting resorption at recharge rates >10 -2.4 km 3 /yr. Through reducing bulk magma compressibility, resorption amplifies pressurization, driving chamber destabilization and potentially expediting eruption onset. Here, we propose that volatile resorption is a natural process both accommodating and promoting rapid chamber pressurization, fundamental to destabilizing large-scale silicic systems. Detecting its signatures in monitoring signals could provide early warning of imminent eruption.
